column1: === EXP-BENCH v3 (v2): Page-Packed Adjacency with Physical IO Counting ===
Cohere 100K, dim=768, k=100, ef=200, W=4, nq=100, warmup=10
NEW: phy/q = physical NVMe reads (miss loads + prefetch loads + bypasses)
NEW: v3-perq-cold = pool.clear() before each query (true single-query cold)
label            ef    k  W   S   D   c%  recall   p50ms   p99ms     qps   exp  blk/q  mis/q  phy/q  hit/q   hit%
label            ef    k  W   S   D   c%  recall   p50ms   p99ms     qps   exp  blk/q  mis/q  phy/q  hit/q   hit%
1. Physical IO (phy/q) — the real NVMe workload:
v1→v3 perq:   151.8 → 135.8 = -11% (pure intra-query reuse, no cross-query warming)
Simulation predicted ~135 unique pages/q with BFS reorder.
This confirms: BFS page packing alone gives ~11% IO reduction from intra-query reuse.
3. Cross-query warming is the bigger win:
v3-cold phy/q = 114.4 (steady-state, no warmup, cross-query cache warms)
Delta: 21.4 reads/q saved by cross-query hot pages (hub region cached)
v1-cold: 322.1 phy/q (!!) — much higher than 201 expansions
This means 322-201 = 121 EXTRA prefetch reads that never became hits
(tiny 32KB pool evicts pages before they can be reused)
v3-cold pool (1MB) retains prefetched pages → prefetch is effective.
v3-perq-cold    8.6   11.8   114.0   (+2% p50 vs v1-warm — intra-query alone is ~break-even)
v3-cold         7.4    9.7   133.4   (-12% p50 vs v1-warm, with NO warmup)
6. Key insight: v3's win comes from TWO sources:
a) Intra-query page reuse (BFS packing, ~11% phy/q reduction)
b) Cross-query hot-page persistence (hub pages stay cached, ~14% more)
c) Effective prefetch (v3 pool large enough to keep prefetched pages)
The combined effect: v3-cold with zero warmup outperforms v1-warm (5% warmup).
7. Recall unchanged: 0.963 for all configs (same graph, same algorithm).
